v:
The Road goes ever on and on
Down from the door where it began.
Now far ahead the Road has gone,
And I must follow, if I can,
src: LotR I.1 / III.? — sung thrice, changing as its singers change
note: Bilbo's at the door, Frodo's on the road, Bilbo's last quiet verse at Rivendell

v:
Upon the hearth the ﬁre is red,
Beneath the roof there is a bed;
But not yet weary are our feet,
Still round the corner we may meet
note: 'Still round the corner there may wait / a new road or a secret gate' — sung again, altered, at the very end

v:
All that is gold does not glitter,
Not all those who wander are lost;
The old that is strong does not wither,
Deep roots are not reached by the frost.
note: Bilbo's verses for Aragorn — Gandalf set them in the letter

v:
Three Rings for the Elven-kings under the sky,
Seven for the Dwarf-lords in their halls of stone,
Nine for Mortal Men doomed to die,
One for the Dark Lord on his dark throne
note: 'the lore of the Elves' — its last two lines were in the Fire

v:
Gil-galad was an Elven-king.
Of him the harpers sadly sing:
the last whose realm was fair and free
between the Mountains and the Sea.
note: Sam speaks it on Weathertop — 'translated... long ago' by Bilbo

t: The Song of Beren and Lúthien
v:
The leaves were long, the grass was green,
The hemlock-umbels tall and fair,
And in the glade a light was seen
Of stars in shadow shimmering.
note: Aragorn chants the ann-thennath under Weathertop, three nights before the knife
Bilbo's audacity — singing of Eärendil in the house of Elrond

v:
An Elven-maid there was of old,
A shining star by day:
Her mantle white was hemmed with gold,
Her shoes of silver-grey.
note: Legolas by the stream that keeps her name

v:
I sang of leaves, of leaves of gold, and leaves of gold there grew:
Of wind I sang, a wind there came and in the branches blew.
Beyond the Sun, beyond the Moon, the foam was on the Sea,
And by the strand of Ilmarin there grew a golden Tree.
note: 'Sang' — before Namárië, in English measure

v:
When evening in the Shire was grey
his footsteps on the Hill were heard;
before the dawn he went away
on journey long without a word.
note: made in Lórien; Sam adds the fireworks stanza

v:
Through Rohan over fen and ﬁeld where the long grass grows
The West Wind comes walking, and about the walls it goes.
‘What news from the West, O wandering wind, do you bring to
me tonight?
note: Aragorn and Legolas sing the West and South winds; the East is left unsung

v:
Where now the horse and the rider? Where is the horn that was
blowing?
Where is the helm and the hauberk, and the bright hair ﬂowing?
Where is the hand on the harpstring, and the red ﬁre glowing?
note: Aragorn recites it before Meduseld — 'thus spoke a forgotten poet'

v:
In the willow-meads of Tasarinan I walked in the Spring.
Ah! the sight and the smell of the Spring in Nan-tasarion!
And I said that was good.
I wandered in Summer in the elm-woods of Ossiriand.
note: the long slow roll of the Entish memory of lost lands

v:
O Orofarnë, Lassemista, Carnimı́rië!
O rowan fair, upon your hair how white the blossom lay!
O rowan mine, I saw you shine upon a summer’s day,
Your rind so bright, your leaves so light, your voice so cool and soft:
note: Quickbeam for his trees

v:
To Isengard! Though Isengard be ringed and barred with doors of
stone;
Though Isengard be strong and hard, as cold as stone and bare as
bone,
note: 'we go, we go, we go to war, to hew the stone and break the door!'

v:
Arise, arise, Riders of Théoden!
Fell deeds awake: ﬁre and slaughter!
spear shall be shaken, shield be splintered,
a sword-day, a red day, ere the sun rises!
note: the horn-call before the Pelennor — 'spear shall be shaken, shield be splintered'

v:
Out of doubt, out of dark to the day’s rising
I came singing in the sun, sword unsheathing.
To hope’s end I rode and to heart’s breaking:
Now for wrath, now for ruin and a red nightfall!
note: laughing amid the wreck, 'and hope he rekindled'

v:
We heard of the horns in the hills ringing,
the swords shining in the South-kingdom.
Steeds went striding to the Stoningland
as wind in the morning. War was kindled.
note: the made-long-afterward song naming the fallen of the Pelennor

v:
Over the land there lies a long shadow,
westward reaching wings of darkness.
The Tower trembles; to the tombs of kings
doom approaches. The Dead awaken;
note: the prophecy of the Paths of the Dead: 'the Dead awaken; for the hour is come for the oathbreakers'

v:
Seek for the Sword that was broken:
In Imladris it dwells;
There shall be counsels taken
Stronger than Morgul-spells.
note: that brought Boromir 110 days' riding to Rivendell

v:
In western lands beneath the Sun
the ﬂowers may rise in Spring,
the trees may bud, the waters run,
the merry ﬁnches sing.
note: 'above all shadows rides the Sun' — and the answering voice was Frodo's

v:
Sing now, ye people of the Tower of Anor,
for the Realm of Sauron is ended for ever,
and the Dark Tower is thrown down.

Sing and rejoice, ye people of the Tower of Guard,
note: the annunciation of victory to Minas Tirith

v:
The world was young, the mountains green,
No stain yet on the Moon was seen,
No words were laid on stream or stone
When Durin woke and walked alone.
note: Gimli chants in the dark of Moria: 'the world is grey, the mountains old'

v:
Troll sat alone on his seat of stone,
And munched and mumbled a bare old bone;
For many a year he had gnawed it near,
For meat was hard to come by.
note: Sam's nonsense — 'to show he had risen to the occasion'

v:
Grey as a mouse,
Big as a house,
Nose like a snake,
I make the earth shake,
note: Sam's hearthside rhyme, spoken with a mûmak in view

v:
Far over the misty mountains cold
To dungeons deep and caverns old
We must away ere break of day
To seek the pale enchanted gold.
note: 'to seek the pale enchanted gold' — the song that woke the Took in Bilbo

v:
Chip the glasses and crack the plates!
Blunt the knives and bend the forks!
That’s what Bilbo Baggins hates–
Smash the bottles and burn the corks!
note: the washing-up assault on Bag End

v:
The King beneath the mountains,
The King of carven stone,
The lord of silver fountains
Shall come into his own!
note: Lake-town sings the old hope; 'the streams shall run in gladness'

t: The Man in the Moon Stayed Up Too Late
v:
There is an inn, a merry old inn
beneath an old grey hill,
And there they brew a beer so brown
That the Man in the Moon himself came down
src: Adventures of Tom Bombadil №5; sung by Frodo at Bree (LotR I.9)
note: the full nursery-rhyme behind 'the cow jumped over the moon'

v:
There was a merry passenger,
a messenger, a mariner:
he built a gilded gondola
to wander in, and had in her
note: the tumbling metre Bilbo later turned to Eärendil's lay

v:
The shadows where the Mewlips dwell
Are dark and wet as ink,
And slow and softly rings their bell,
As in the slime you sink.
note: the Shire's one true horror-poem

v:
The fat cat on the mat
may seem to dream
of nice mice that suffice
for him, or cream;
note: Sam's? — 'may seem to dream'; claws in the dream

v:
I walked by the sea, and there came to me,
as a star-beam on the wet sand,
a white shell like a sea-bell;
trembling it lay in my wet hand.
note: the darkest leaf in the Red Book's margins

t: The Lay of Leithian — Canto I
v:
A king there was in days of old:
ere Men yet walked upon the mould
his power was reared in cavern's shade,
his hand was over glen and glade.
src: HoME III, The Lays of Beleriand
note: the great rhymed telling of Beren and Lúthien; 'ere Men yet walked upon the mould'

t: The Lay of the Children of Húrin — opening
v:
Lo! the golden dragon of the God of Hell,
the gloom of the woods of the world now gone,
the woes of Men, and weeping of Elves
fading faintly down forest pathways,
note: Tolkien's long alliterative line: 'Lo! the golden dragon...'
